meta.name: Unstable Supraventricular Tachycardia — Synchronized Cardioversion with Procedural Sedation

patient.name: Marcus Webb
patient.chiefComplaint: Sudden onset of palpitations 30 minutes ago at the gym, now with chest pressure, severe lightheadedness, near-syncope, and inability to stand without holding the wall.

history.hpi: 45-year-old male was on the treadmill at his neighborhood gym when he suddenly felt his heart 'go off' — palpitations he describes as 'something jumped inside my chest' followed by a sustained very rapid heart rate. He sat down, but unlike his prior episodes, this one is not stopping on its own. Within 15 minutes he became diaphoretic, light-headed, developed substernal chest pressure rated 5/10, and once nearly fainted when trying to walk to the lobby. Patient has had two prior episodes diagnosed as PSVT in the past 4 years that resolved with vagal maneuvers. He is otherwise healthy, no known structural heart disease, no medications.
pastMedical: Two prior episodes of paroxysmal SVT (resolved with vagal maneuvers)
history.lastOralIntake: Pre-workout drink (with caffeine) about 90 minutes ago; protein bar 2 hours ago
history.events: Sudden onset of palpitations on the treadmill ~30 minutes prior to EMS arrival; progressive symptoms — chest pressure, diaphoresis, lightheadedness, near-syncope; gym staff called 911.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 45-year-old male, complaint of rapid heart rate and chest pressure at the gym. Conscious. Stage 1 response.
scene.location: Commercial gym, lobby couch where staff helped him sit. Adequate lighting; clear path to door for cot.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Gym staff cooperative; other members keeping respectful distance.
scene.bystanders: Gym manager and front-desk staff. Confirm patient was on treadmill, suddenly stopped, sat down, became pale and short of breath. No witnessed loss of consciousness, no fall.
visualCues: Athletic male in workout clothes seated on lobby couch, leaning forward
Hand pressed against mid-chest, Levine sign
Water bottle on the floor beside him
Smartwatch on wrist showing HR alert at 195+

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS arrives to find a 45-year-old male in clear distress: pale, diaphoretic, with substernal chest pressure and lightheadedness. Initial monitor connection shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 190 — supraventricular tachycardia. Importantly, his blood pressure is 78/52 with worsening mentation and chest pain — this is unstable SVT. The teaching point is the rapid pivot from any pharmacologic plan straight to synchronized cardioversion, with procedural sedation/analgesia for the responsive patient.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Rapid weak radial pulse — too fast to count manually; cool, pale, diaphoretic; cap refill 3 sec
otherFindings: Regular narrow complex tachycardia at 190 — SVT
Two near-syncopal episodes in past 15 minutes
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: It hasn't stopped this time. I feel like I'm going to pass out. I had it before but the bearing-down trick worked then.

expectedActions.shortAction: Primary survey; assess stability — chest pain + AMS + hypotension = UNSTABLE
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Patient Presentation — instability defined by hemodynamic compromise, AMS, chest pain, syncope/near-syncope.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patients will manifest elevated heart rate for age and may or may not also present with associated signs or symptoms such as palpitations, dyspnea, chest pain, syncope/near-syncope, hemodynamic compromise, altered mental status, or other signs of end organ malperfusion.

expectedActions.shortAction: O2 NC → NRB titrate to SpO2 94–98%
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Adult Management 1–2 — manage airway, oxygen targeted 94–98%.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adult Management: Manage airway as necessary. Administer oxygen as appropriate with a target of achieving 94–98% saturation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Cardiac monitor; defib pads on (sync mode pre-armed); 12-lead EKG
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia step 3 — monitor + 12-lead; pre-arming sync mode minimizes time to cardioversion.

expectedActions.shortAction: IV access (large-bore proximal — for sedation + post-conversion meds)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia step 4 — IV access required for medication and sedation administration.

expectedActions.shortAction: Check blood glucose — rule out hypoglycemia driver
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Check blood glucose and treat hypoglycemia per the Hypoglycemia Guideline.

expectedActions.shortAction: Classify rhythm: regular narrow complex = SVT; unstable → cardiovert (skip pharmacology)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Regular Narrow Unstable — cardioversion is the immediate intervention; do not delay with adenosine or vagal in unstable patient.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Regular Narrow Complex Tachycardia – Unstable: Deliver a synchronized shock based on manufacturer's recommendations. For responsive patients, consider sedation and analgesia.

transitions.condition: Crew administers procedural sedation (fentanyl + low-dose midazolam) followed by synchronized cardioversion at biphasic 100 J; rhythm converts to sinus.
Crew cardioverts without giving sedation/analgesia first; alert patient experiences extreme pain and anxiety, lasting psychological distress.

phases.name: Procedural sedation → sync cardioversion
phases.description: Crew explains the procedure. Procedural sedation given: fentanyl 85 mcg IV (85 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 85 mcg, max 100 mcg) for analgesia, plus midazolam 2 mg IV slow push for amnesia/anxiolysis (small dose given hemodynamic instability — half the typical max). Pre-oxygenated, monitored, and synchronized cardioversion delivered at biphasic 100 J. Rhythm converts to sinus rhythm at 86 bpm with prompt restoration of BP and resolution of chest pain.
phases.triggerCondition: Pre-shock procedural sedation (fentanyl + low-dose midazolam) administered followed by synchronized cardioversion at biphasic energy; rhythm converted to sinus.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong regular pulse at 86 post-conversion; warm and dry
otherFindings: Pre-shock fentanyl 85 mcg + midazolam 2 mg given
Rhythm converted to sinus at 86 bpm
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (drowsy, slow speech): I felt the shock but it wasn't bad. Thank you for the medicine first. I feel a lot better.

expectedActions.shortAction: Fentanyl 85 mcg IV (85 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) — analgesia
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 6b — fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV/IM/IN/IO max 100 mcg; analgesia for procedure.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For Moderate to Severe pain, analgesics include: Fentanyl: 1 mcg/kg IN, IM, IV or IO (maximum initial dose of 100 mcg).

expectedActions.shortAction: Midazolam 2 mg slow IV push — amnesia/anxiolysis (small dose given hypotension)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tox Universal Tx — adult midazolam 0.1 mg/kg in 2 mg increments slow IV push, max 5 mg; reduce by 50% in elderly (≥69). Use small initial increment with marginal BP.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adult: midazolam 0.1 mg/kg in 2 mg increments slow IV push over one to two minutes per increment with maximum single dose 5 mg — reduce by 50% for patients 69 years old or older.

expectedActions.shortAction: Allergies, BP, SpO2 confirmed; capnography on (capno predicts hypoventilation early)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 7 + Patient Safety #1, #2 — capnography earlier predictor of hypoventilation; caution with opioids in hypotension/hypoxia.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Use of non-invasive capnography is an earlier predictor of hypoventilation than pulse oximetry if opioid medications are administered. Patient Safety Considerations: Administer opioids with caution to patients with Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) less than 15, hypotension, identified medication allergy, hypoxia (SPO2 less than 90%) after maximal supplemental oxygen therapy, or signs of hypoventilation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Synchronized cardioversion 100 J biphasic; reassess pulse/rhythm post-shock
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Regular Narrow Unstable — synchronized shock per manufacturer for unstable SVT.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Regular Narrow Complex Tachycardia – Unstable: Deliver a synchronized shock based on manufacturer's recommendations. For responsive patients, consider sedation and analgesia.

expectedActions.shortAction: Post-shock: confirm rhythm, pulse, BP; serial reassessment of mentation and pain
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Documentation — initial rhythm, all rhythm changes, time/dose/response of meds, monitor strips after each intervention.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Initial rhythm and all rhythm changes; Time, dose, and response to medications given; Cardioversion times, synchronization, attempts, joules, and response; Obtain monitor strips after each intervention; Patient weight.

transitions.condition: Rhythm converted to sinus; hemodynamics restored; transport for cardiology evaluation; monitor for recurrence and post-sedation respiratory status.

phases.name: Sinus rhythm; transport
phases.description: Patient transported to cardiology-capable receiving hospital with continuous monitoring. Mentation clearing as sedation wears off. SpO2 and EtCO2 monitored continuously for opioid/benzo-induced respiratory depression. Watch for SVT recurrence en route. Bedside handoff includes initial rhythm, sedation given, joules delivered, conversion documented.
phases.triggerCondition: Rhythm converted to sinus; hemodynamics restored; transport with continued monitoring of mentation and respiratory status during sedation wear-off.

otherFindings: Mentation clearing as sedation wears off
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I feel pretty good now. I'm a little drowsy but okay. Thanks again.

expectedActions.shortAction: Continuous monitor for SVT recurrence; pads stay on; pre-arm sync mode
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia — initial rhythm and changes documented; pads remain in place for any recurrence.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Initial rhythm and all rhythm changes; Time, dose, and response to medications given.

expectedActions.shortAction: Continuous SpO2 + capnography during sedation wear-off; reassess pain/RR q5 min
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 7 + Documentation — capnography earlier predictor of opioid hypoventilation; reassess.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Use of non-invasive capnography is an earlier predictor of hypoventilation than pulse oximetry if opioid medications are administered. Documentation of patient reassessment with repeat vital signs and patient pain scale assessment.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport to cardiology-capable hospital; bedside handoff with strips + dose timeline
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Documentation — full handoff with rhythm strips, sedation, cardioversion details.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Cardioversion times, synchronization, attempts, joules, and response. Obtain monitor strips after each intervention. Patient weight.

phases.name: Cardioversion without sedation
phases.description: Crew cardioverted without giving any procedural sedation or analgesia. The shock was effective and the rhythm converted to sinus, but the alert patient experienced the full pain of the shock. He yells, becomes anxious and tearful, and the experience leaves a lasting psychological imprint that may affect his willingness to seek care for future episodes. The protocol explicitly says 'For responsive patients, consider sedation and analgesia' — this is the moment to do it, not after.
phases.triggerCondition: Synchronized cardioversion delivered without pre-shock sedation/analgesia in an alert patient; rhythm converted but patient suffered significant pain and psychological distress.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong regular pulse at 88 post-conversion; warm
otherFindings: Rhythm converted to sinus — clinical objective met
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Why didn't you give me anything?! That was the worst pain of my life! I want to go home!

expectedActions.shortAction: Fentanyl 85 mcg IV NOW (85 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) for residual chest wall pain
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 6b — fentanyl 1 mcg/kg max 100 mcg; reactive analgesia for post-cardioversion chest pain.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For Moderate to Severe pain, analgesics include: Fentanyl: 1 mcg/kg IN, IM, IV or IO (maximum initial dose of 100 mcg). If indicated based on pain assessment, and vital signs allow, repeat pain medication administration (excluding acetaminophen and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medicines) after 5 minutes of the previous dose.

expectedActions.shortAction: Verbal reassurance; explain procedure and outcome; non-pharmacologic measures
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 4 — non-pharmacologic options including verbal reassurance.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Non-pharmacologic pain management options include: Placement of the patient in a position of comfort. Application of ice packs and/or splints for pain secondary to trauma. Verbal reassurance to control anxiety.

expectedActions.shortAction: Encourage transport for cardiology evaluation despite distress
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Patient Care Goals — restoration of sinus achieved but cardiology evaluation needed for SVT recurrence prevention.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Care Goals: Restore regular sinus rhythm in unstable patient. Search for underlying cause.

expectedActions.shortAction: Document plan: pre-shock sedation/analgesia in future cardioversions; QI moment
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Performance Measures — use of sedation for responsive patient; explicitly tracked.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance Measures: Time to clinical improvement from patient contact. Blood sugar obtained. Correct medication(s) and dose given for patient condition, age, and weight. Correct cardioversion joules delivered given patient weight and/or condition. Use of sedation for responsive patient.
